seo.title: Koh Samui: Complete Travel Guide 2026 — Top Things To Do
seo.metaDescription: Plan your Koh Samui trip with our 2026 guide. Best beaches, temples, island tours, hotels from budget to luxury, and insider tips for Thailand's Gulf island.

overview: Thailand's second-largest island sits in the Gulf of Thailand off Surat Thani province, offering a tropical mix of palm-fringed beaches, coconut plantations, luxury resorts, and jungle-covered mountains. Unlike Phuket's Andaman coast energy, Koh Samui delivers a more laid-back island vibe — from the bustling Chaweng strip to the bohemian Fisherman's Village in Bophut and the sleepy shores of Maenam. The island is a gateway to Ang Thong Marine Park (42 islands), Koh Phangan (Full Moon Party), and Koh Tao (diving). Bangkok Airways operates the private Samui Airport (USM), or budget travelers can fly to Surat Thani and take a ferry from Don Sak pier.
highlights: Chaweng Beach — Koh Samui's longest and liveliest beach with bars, clubs, and water sports
Big Buddha Temple (Wat Phra Yai) — iconic 12-meter golden Buddha statue visible from the air on approach to Samui Airport
Ang Thong Marine National Park — a stunning archipelago of 42 islands accessible by speedboat day trip
Fisherman's Village, Bophut — charming converted Chinese shophouses with boutique restaurants and a famous Friday night market
Na Muang Waterfalls — two jungle waterfalls with natural swimming pools in the island's mountainous interior
Secret Buddha Garden (Tarnim Magic Garden) — a hidden sculpture park on top of Pom Mountain with panoramic views
Hin Ta & Hin Yai (Grandfather & Grandmother Rocks) — famous natural rock formations on the southern coast near Lamai Beach
Wat Plai Laem — ornate lakeside temple with an 18-armed Guanyin statue and fish-filled ponds
Gulf island hopping — easy ferry connections to Koh Phangan (30 min) and Koh Tao (2 hours) for diving
Year-round warm weather with peak season December to March and a unique monsoon cycle offset from the Andaman coast

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Dec–Mar: Dry season with sunshine, calm seas, and comfortable temperatures around 28–32°C. February and March are the driest months with clear blue skies. Peak tourist season with highest hotel prices, especially around Christmas and New Year.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Apr–Sep: Hot season transitioning to early monsoon. April–June remain warm and sunny but quieter, with hotel prices 30–40% below peak. Best conditions for snorkeling and diving. July–September sees occasional afternoon showers but plenty of sunshine between.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Oct–Nov: The wettest months, with November averaging the most rainfall. Seas can be rough and some boat tours to Ang Thong are suspended. However, the island turns lush green, crowds thin dramatically, and luxury resorts offer their lowest rates.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit December to February for guaranteed beach weather and calm seas. For the best value with good weather, May and June are the sweet spot — warm, sunny, and 30–40% cheaper than peak season. Koh Samui's monsoon season is offset from Phuket's, making it a good November alternative when the Andaman coast is wet.

topAttractions.name: Big Buddha Temple (Wat Phra Yai)
topAttractions.description: Koh Samui's most iconic landmark — a 12-meter tall golden seated Buddha statue on a small island (Koh Fan) connected to the northeast coast by a causeway. Built in 1972, the temple is visible from the air when landing at Samui Airport. Surrounding the main statue are smaller shrines, bell towers, and souvenir stalls.
topAttractions.location: Koh Fan, northeast coast near Bophut
tips: Open daily 7 AM – 6:30 PM
Dress modestly — free sarongs available at the entrance stairs
Visit early morning to avoid tour groups and midday heat

topAttractions.name: Ang Thong Marine National Park
topAttractions.description: A breathtaking archipelago of 42 limestone islands about 35 km northwest of Koh Samui. Activities include kayaking through emerald lagoons, hiking to panoramic viewpoints, snorkeling over coral reefs, and swimming in the famous Emerald Lake (Thale Nai) — a saltwater lake hidden inside one of the islands. Full-day tours by speedboat or catamaran depart from Nathon pier.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Full-day tours 1,800–3,500 THB (~$50–100 USD) including national park fee (300 THB), lunch, and equipment
tips: Book the speedboat option for more time on the islands and less time in transit
The park is occasionally closed during November–December monsoon — check ahead
Bring reef-safe sunscreen and water shoes for the rocky beaches

topAttractions.name: Wat Plai Laem
topAttractions.description: A colorful lakeside temple complex featuring a striking 18-armed Guanyin (Goddess of Mercy) statue rising from the water, alongside a large laughing Buddha statue. The temple sits on stilts over a fish-filled lake where visitors can buy fish food (20 THB). A blend of Thai and Chinese architectural styles with intricate murals inside the main hall.
tips: Open 7 AM – 6 PM daily
Combine with Big Buddha — only 5 minutes away by car
The fish feeding is a fun activity for children

topAttractions.name: Na Muang Waterfalls
topAttractions.description: Two jungle waterfalls in the mountainous interior of Koh Samui. Na Muang 1 drops 18 meters into a large natural swimming pool — easily accessible by a short walk from the car park. Na Muang 2 is taller at 80 meters but requires a 30-minute jungle hike through sometimes slippery terrain. Both are surrounded by lush tropical vegetation.
topAttractions.location: Central-south Koh Samui, Na Muang district
tips: Na Muang 1 is family-friendly with an easy path; Na Muang 2 requires proper footwear for the jungle hike
Best visited after rains when the waterfalls are fullest (Oct–Jan)
Avoid the elephant camps near the parking area — choose ethical sanctuaries instead

topAttractions.name: Hin Ta & Hin Yai (Grandfather & Grandmother Rocks)
topAttractions.description: Famous naturally-shaped rock formations on the rocky shore south of Lamai Beach. The rocks resemble male and female genitalia, making them one of Koh Samui's most photographed — and giggled-at — natural landmarks. A short boardwalk leads to viewpoints, with small food stalls and souvenir shops nearby.
tips: Quick 15-minute visit — combine with Lamai Beach exploration
Best light for photos in the morning

topAttractions.name: Secret Buddha Garden (Tarnim Magic Garden)
topAttractions.description: A hidden sculpture garden on the top of Pom Mountain (at 460 meters), created in 1976 by a retired Samui fruit farmer named Nim Thongsuk. The garden features stone statues of deities, animals, and human figures set among natural jungle, streams, and small waterfalls. The journey up the steep dirt road is an adventure in itself — 4WD required.
tips: Only accessible by 4WD vehicle — the final 2 km is a steep, unpaved track
Book a safari-style 4WD tour that combines the garden with Na Muang Waterfalls
Bring mosquito repellent and water

topAttractions.name: Fisherman's Village, Bophut
topAttractions.description: A charming strip of converted Chinese-Thai shophouses along Bophut Beach's waterfront, now housing boutique hotels, restaurants, art galleries, and cocktail bars. The famous Friday Walking Street Market (5–11 PM) transforms the village with food stalls, live music, fire dancers, and handmade crafts. It's Samui's most atmospheric neighborhood.
tips: Friday Walking Street Market (5–11 PM) is the island's best night market
Great sunset views from the beachfront restaurants
Book dinner reservations at popular restaurants like Zazen on weekends

topAttractions.name: Wat Khunaram (Mummified Monk)
topAttractions.description: A Buddhist temple famous for housing the mummified body of Luang Pho Daeng, a revered monk who died in 1973 while meditating. His body has been remarkably preserved and is displayed in a glass case wearing sunglasses. The temple compound is peaceful and uncrowded, offering a unique and somewhat surreal cultural experience.
tips: A 15-minute visit — combine with a trip to Na Muang Waterfalls nearby
Photography is permitted but be respectful
Open daily 9 AM – 5 PM

topAttractions.name: Chaweng Beach
topAttractions.description: Koh Samui's longest and most popular beach stretches 7 km along the east coast with soft white sand and turquoise waters. The beach is divided into North Chaweng (calmer), Central Chaweng (buzzing with beach bars and water sports), and Chaweng Noi (quieter, more upscale). Behind the beach, Chaweng Walking Street is the island's main nightlife strip.
tips: Sunbeds available for 100–200 THB or free with a drink purchase at beach bars
North Chaweng is quieter; central Chaweng has the most action
Avoid swimming at the southern rocky end

travelTips: Koh Samui has its own airport (USM) operated privately by Bangkok Airways — flights are convenient but pricier than mainland airports. Budget alternative: fly to Surat Thani and take a ferry from Don Sak pier (1.5 hours).
Rent a scooter (200–350 THB/day) for maximum freedom — the ring road circles the island in about 1 hour. International driving permit technically required.
Tap water is not drinkable. Buy large bottles from 7-Eleven (15–20 THB) rather than small tourist-priced bottles.
The island has two Tesco Lotus and several Big C stores — stock up on snacks and supplies at local prices rather than tourist-area convenience stores.
Koh Samui's monsoon season (Oct–Nov) is offset from Phuket and the Andaman coast. When Phuket gets rain in May–October, Samui is often sunny.
Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run along the ring road for 40–100 THB per person. Agree on price before boarding.
ATMs charge 220 THB per withdrawal regardless of bank. Withdraw larger amounts less frequently to minimize fees.
Book Ang Thong Marine Park tours at least one day ahead in high season — they sell out, especially speedboat options.
Full Moon Party on Koh Phangan is a 30-minute ferry ride away. Boats run late into the night on party nights (500–600 THB return).
gettingThere: Samui Airport (USM) receives direct Bangkok Airways flights from Bangkok Suvarnabhumi (1 hour, 3,500–8,000 THB one-way). For budget travelers, fly AirAsia or Nok Air to Surat Thani (URT), then take a shuttle bus to Don Sak pier (1 hour) and a Seatran Discovery or Lomprayah ferry to Koh Samui (1.5 hours). Combined flight+ferry packages from Surat Thani cost 1,000–2,000 THB total. Overnight buses from Bangkok's Southern Terminal take 12–14 hours with ferry included (700–1,000 THB). Trains run to Surat Thani station with onward ferry connection (from 500 THB, 14 hours total).
gettingAround: Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) follow the ring road for 40–100 THB. Scooter rental is 200–350 THB/day and the most popular way to explore. Grab is available but less reliable than on the mainland. Taxi meters don't exist — negotiate fixed prices. Car rental from 800 THB/day for families. The ring road (Route 4169/4170) circles the island in about an hour.
